summary: DHH dismantles the claim that Rails is a bloated monolith by methodically examining its actual lines of code and modular architecture. He shows that naive LOC measurements overcount by a factor of 20 when they include comments, whitespace, tests, and bundled dependencies. He then demonstrates that Rails' six major components can be individually excluded, and even within Action Pack, optional features are cleanly separated via mixins using alias_method_chain. His conclusion is that modularity should mean code is understandable in isolation, not that frameworks must ship as puzzles for developers to assemble.
key_points: Naive LOC measurements of Rails overcount by up to 20x by including comments, whitespace, tests, and bundled dependencies
Rails' six major components (Action Mailer, Active Resource, Active Support, Active Record, Action Pack, Rail Ties) can be individually included or excluded
Action Controller splits roughly in half between essential (~3,800 lines) and optional (~3,500 lines) modules that can be removed by commenting out includes
alias_method_chain enables clean modularity by letting optional modules decorate core methods without the core knowing about them
True modularity means code is understandable in isolation — not that it must be delivered as a disassembled puzzle
money_quotes: Most measurements of LOCs apply hardly any rigor and reduces what could otherwise be a somewhat useful indicator to an inverse dick measurement match.
So the difference between committing all the mistakes and reality is a factor of 20.

The important part about being modular is that the pieces are understandable in isolation. That the individual modules have coherence and cohesion. Not that they're actually handed to you as a puzzle for you to figure out how to put together.
I'd much rather give someone a complete picture, which they can then turn into a puzzle if they're so inclined.
ActionController::Base declares render and perform_action, but doesn't know anything about benchmarking (why should it?).
key_insight: Rails is not a monolith but a well-organized collection of modular components — and shipping a complete, coherent package is better than forcing developers to assemble the pieces themselves.
